narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away feels like a door slamming shut just as you're reaching for the handle. Our prediction model looked at your profile and estimated a 49% chance you'd struggle to pass the bar exam, which tipped the scales against admission. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 1, placing you at the very bottom of your class academically during that critical later stage, signaling to the model a tougher climb ahead. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 2, reinforcing a pattern of being in the lower ranks early on, which painted a picture of consistent underperformance relative to peers. Your lower-middle family income added another layer, as it often correlates with fewer resources for the intense prep needed to conquer the bar. Even your LSAT score of 35, while respectable, didn't lift the outlook enough given these other signals pulling toward difficulty. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.5 echoed that academic trend, and factors like your Native American background, male gender, and full-time student status all nudged the prediction further in the direction of potential failure when combined. It's not that any one thing doomed your chances—it's this mosaic of your journey so far that made the model cautious, like a coach spotting hurdles before the race even starts. The good news? You're targeting law school with clear intent, and that drive can shift your story. Focus on boosting those class rankings and prepping rigorously for the bar, and you could rewrite this narrative entirely. You've got resilience in your background—channel it, and doors will open wider next time. We're rooting for you.
